reasoning:
The question asks whether CPT's unadjusted close price will be higher than its Mar 30 (Mar 28 close) baseline on five future dates. The key insight lies in CPT's quarterly dividend schedule. CPT pays a relatively high dividend (~3.5% yield) which causes its nominal share price to drop slightly on ex-dividend dates, acting as a headwind against capital appreciation.

Specifically, CPT recently announced a $1.05 dividend with an ex-dividend date of Monday, March 31, 2025. Because the base price is established on March 28 (the previous Friday), all subsequent resolution prices will measure against a baseline that does not include this ~$1.05 immediate markdown. 

- For April 6: The price will have dropped by ~0.87% due to the dividend on Mar 31. The few days of expected market drift are not enough to recover this drop, placing the expected return firmly negative. (Estimated p = 0.41)
- For April 29: After one month, natural price drift recovers some of the dividend gap, but the median expectation remains slightly below the starting price. (Estimated p = 0.46)
- For June 28 (resolving Jun 27): Historical patterns for Q2 ex-dates dictate that it generally hits on the final day of the quarter (June 30). This allows three months of market drift to completely overcome the Q1 dividend hit right before the next drop happens. (Estimated p = 0.51)
- For Sept 26: Similarly, Q3's ex-dividend likely falls on Sept 30, meaning six months of equity drift only needs to overcome Q1+Q2 dividend payouts, giving a slight positive median. (Estimated p = 0.52)
- For March 30, 2026: One year out, general risk premium expectations push the median unadjusted stock price higher, and the 2026 Q1 dividend likely won't hit until March 31. (Estimated p = 0.54)
